name.text: Week 15 – Q4: Radiation Pattern and Antenna Efficiency

questiontext.text:
Scenario: Your team is developing a compact wireless sensor for an industrial monitoring system. The sensor uses a Hertzian dipole antenna of length \(\ell = {@l_cm@}\) cm operating at \(f = {@f_GHz@}\) GHz in free space. The antenna wire has an ohmic loss resistance \(R_{\text{loss}} = {@R_loss@}\;\Omega\).

You need to evaluate the antenna's efficiency, visualize its radiation pattern in MATLAB, and assess whether this antenna is suitable for the intended application.

Part A — Radiation Efficiency

(a) Calculate the radiation resistance \(R_{\text{rad}} = 80\pi^2(\ell/\lambda)^2\).

\(R_{\text{rad}} = \) [[input:ans1]] [[validation:ans1]] \(\Omega\)

Enter in ohms, e.g., 42.0.

(b) Calculate the radiation efficiency — the fraction of input power that is actually radiated (rather than lost as heat in the wire): \(\xi = \dfrac{R_{\text{rad}}}{R_{\text{rad}} + R_{\text{loss}}}\).

\(\xi = \) [[input:ans2]] [[validation:ans2]]

Enter as a decimal between 0 and 1, e.g., 0.29.

(c) Calculate the antenna gain \(G = \xi \times D\), where \(D = 1.5\) for a Hertzian dipole. Express in dBi.

\(G = \) [[input:ans3]] [[validation:ans3]] dBi

Gain can be negative in dBi when efficiency is low, e.g., 4.5.

Part B — MATLAB Radiation Pattern

Complete the MATLAB code below to plot the normalized power radiation pattern \(F(\theta) = \sin^2\theta\) as a polar plot.

theta = 0:0.01:_____;          % ans4: upper bound of angular range (radians)
F = _____;                     % ans5: normalized power pattern as function of theta
polarplot(theta, F)
title('Hertzian Dipole — Normalized Power Pattern')

% The half-power beamwidth (HPBW) angles occur where F = 0.5.
% Solving sin^2(theta) = 0.5:
theta_hpbw_1 = _____;          % ans6: first HPBW angle (radians, 0 < theta < pi)
theta_hpbw_2 = _____;          % ans7: second HPBW angle (radians, 0 < theta < pi)

(d) Upper bound of theta range for a full polar plot (in radians):

[[input:ans4]] [[validation:ans4]]

Use %pi for π, e.g., 3*%pi.

(e) Normalized power pattern expression \(F(\theta)\) as a function of theta:

[[input:ans5]] [[validation:ans5]]

Use sin(theta), cos(theta), and ^ for powers, e.g., cos(theta)^2.

(f) First HPBW angle \(\theta_1\) (in radians, between 0 and \(\pi\)):

[[input:ans6]] [[validation:ans6]]

Use %pi for π, e.g., %pi/6.

(g) Second HPBW angle \(\theta_2\) (in radians, between 0 and \(\pi\)):

[[input:ans7]] [[validation:ans7]]

Use %pi for π, e.g., 5*%pi/6.

Part C — Design Interpretation

(h) The sensor needs to communicate with a receiver located directly above (along the antenna's axis, \(\theta = 0°\)). Based on the radiation pattern you characterized in Part B, is this a good antenna orientation for that link?

[[input:ans8]] [[validation:ans8]]

(i) The gain you computed in part (c) is negative in dBi. What does a negative gain (in dBi) mean physically?

[[input:ans9]] [[validation:ans9]]

generalfeedback.text:
Worked Solution

Given: \(\ell = {@l_cm@}\) cm, \(f = {@f_GHz@}\) GHz, \(R_{\text{loss}} = {@R_loss@}\;\Omega\).

(a) \(\lambda = c/f = {@lambda_val@}\) m, \(\ell/\lambda = {@ratio_val@}\).

\[R_{\text{rad}} = 80\pi^2\left(\frac{\ell}{\lambda}\right)^2 = 80\pi^2 \times ({@ratio_val@})^2 = {@tans1@}\;\Omega\]

(b) Radiation efficiency:

\[\xi = \frac{R_{\text{rad}}}{R_{\text{rad}} + R_{\text{loss}}} = \frac{{@tans1@}}{{@tans1@} + {@R_loss@}} = {@tans2@}\]

This means only {@float(tans2*100)@}% of the input power is radiated; the rest is lost as heat in the wire.

(c) Gain:

\[G = \xi \times D = {@tans2@} \times 1.5 = {@G_linear@}\]

\[G_{\text{dBi}} = 10\log_{10}({@G_linear@}) = {@tans3@} \text{ dBi}\]

(d)–(g) MATLAB code:

theta = 0:0.01:2*pi;           % full polar plot needs 0 to 2*pi
F = sin(theta).^2;             % normalized power pattern
polarplot(theta, F)

theta_hpbw_1 = pi/4;           % sin^2(pi/4) = 0.5
theta_hpbw_2 = 3*pi/4;         % sin^2(3*pi/4) = 0.5

The HPBW = \(\theta_2 - \theta_1 = 3\pi/4 - \pi/4 = \pi/2 = 90°\).

Physical meaning: the Hertzian dipole radiates maximum power broadside (\(\theta = 90°\)) and zero along its axis (\(\theta = 0°, 180°\)). The half-power beamwidth of 90° means it is a relatively unfocused radiator.

(h) The \(\sin^2\theta\) pattern has a null (zero radiation) at \(\theta = 0°\) — placing a receiver along the antenna axis means no signal arrives, regardless of transmit power or distance. The antenna must be oriented so the receiver is in the broadside direction (\(\theta = 90°\)). This is a common real-world consideration when mounting dipole antennas.

(i) A negative gain in dBi means \(G < 1\) in linear scale — the antenna radiates less power in its peak direction than an ideal isotropic radiator would with the same total input power. Here, \(G = \xi \times 1.5 = {@G_linear@}\) (linear), which is less than 1, giving {@tans3@} dBi. The directivity of 1.5 (1.76 dBi) is not enough to overcome the efficiency losses. For a battery-powered sensor, this means most of the transmit power is wasted as heat rather than being radiated.

questionnote.text: {@sconcat("l_cm=", l_cm, " f_GHz=", f_GHz, " R_loss=", R_loss, " R_rad=", tans1, " xi=", tans2, " G_dBi=", tans3)@}

feedbackvariables.text: err_xi_flag: if abs(xi_err) > 0 and abs(ans2 - xi_err)/abs(xi_err) < 0.06 then 1 else 0;

truefeedback.text: You computed \(R_{\text{rad}}/R_{\text{loss}}\), but efficiency is \(\xi = R_{\text{rad}}/(R_{\text{rad}} + R_{\text{loss}})\). The denominator is the total resistance.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. \(\xi = R_{\text{rad}}/(R_{\text{rad}} + R_{\text{loss}})\). Efficiency must be between 0 and 1.
Incorrect. \(G = \xi \times D = \xi \times 1.5\), then convert to dBi: \(G_{\text{dBi}} = 10\log_{10}(G)\). Note that G can be less than 1 (negative dBi) when efficiency is low.

truefeedback.text: Correct. A full polar plot requires \(\theta\) from 0 to \(2\pi\).

falsefeedback.text: For a complete polar plot, \(\theta\) must sweep from 0 to \(2\pi\). Using only 0 to \(\pi\) would show only the upper half of the pattern.

truefeedback.text: You wrote \(\sin\theta\), which is the field pattern. The question asks for the power pattern, which is \(\sin^2\theta\) (power is proportional to field squared).

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. The Hertzian dipole far-field \(E_\theta \propto \sin\theta\), so the power pattern (proportional to \(|E|^2\)) is \(\sin^2\theta\). Type: sin(theta)^2.

feedbackvariables.text: /* Check if student gave degrees instead of radians */ err_deg_flag: if abs(ans6 - 45) < 2 then 1 else 0;

truefeedback.text: You gave 45°, but MATLAB uses radians. The answer is \(\pi/4\). Type: %pi/4.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. Solve \(\sin^2\theta = 0.5\) for \(\theta\) in the range \((0, \pi)\). The first solution is \(\theta = \pi/4\).

truefeedback.text: You gave 135°, but the answer should be in radians: \(3\pi/4\). Type: 3*%pi/4.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. The second solution to \(\sin^2\theta = 0.5\) in \((0,\pi)\) is \(\theta = 3\pi/4\).

truefeedback.text: Correct. The \(\sin^2\theta\) pattern has a null at \(\theta = 0°\) — placing a receiver along the axis means no signal arrives. Orient the antenna so the receiver is broadside (\(\theta = 90°\)).

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. Evaluate the pattern at \(\theta = 0°\): \(F(0) = \sin^2(0) = 0\). Zero radiation along the axis — this is the worst possible orientation for that receiver.

truefeedback.text: Correct. Negative dBi means \(G < 1\) — ohmic losses outweigh the directivity advantage, so this antenna performs worse than a lossless isotropic radiator. For battery-powered sensors, this means most transmit power is wasted as heat.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. Negative dBi means \(G < 1\) in linear scale. Gain = efficiency × directivity. When efficiency is low (high losses), the gain drops below the directivity, and if low enough, below 0 dBi. The antenna still radiates — it just radiates less than an ideal isotropic source.

hint.text: Efficiency: Compute \(\lambda = c/f\), then \(\ell/\lambda\), then \(R_{\text{rad}} = 80\pi^2(\ell/\lambda)^2\). Think of \(R_{\text{rad}}\) and \(R_{\text{loss}}\) as two resistors sharing the current: the fraction of power that goes into \(R_{\text{rad}}\) is radiated, and the fraction that goes into \(R_{\text{loss}}\) is lost as heat. If efficiency is below 1, the gain will be less than the directivity.

MATLAB pattern: polarplot needs \(\theta\) from 0 to \(2\pi\) for a full pattern. The power pattern is \(\sin^2\theta\) (not \(\sin\theta\) — that is the field pattern, and power is proportional to field squared). The pattern shows where radiation goes: maximum broadside (\(\theta = 90°\)), zero along the axis. Think about where you would NOT want to place a receiver.

HPBW and interpretation: The half-power angles solve \(\sin^2\theta = 0.5\), giving \(\sin\theta = 1/\sqrt{2}\), so \(\theta_1 = \pi/4\) and \(\theta_2 = 3\pi/4\) (in radians). For Part C: consider what the pattern means for placing a receiver along the axis, and what it means when gain drops below 0 dBi.
